---
title: "Responsible AI"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} · **Governance, Ethics & Equity** track · ~35 min hands-on + readings and quiz
:::

[← Back to the Governance, Ethics & Equity track](governance-ethics.qmd){.track-back}

*Pull the ethics thread together: who's accountable, what to disclose, and how to govern AI use.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Apply the accountability principle: AI drafts, a human decides
- Decide when to disclose AI use
- Use a governance framework (WHO, ASPPH) to guide decisions

## Overview

Responsible AI isn't a separate task — it's how you do every task. The governing principle from Workshop 1 holds: the person who acts on AI output owns the result. Disclosure, verification, and human oversight are the operational habits.

For grants and manuscripts, this principle now has real regulatory teeth: NIH will not review applications substantially developed by AI, NSF requires disclosure in the project description, and ICMJE/COPE prohibit AI authorship — see [Grant Proposal Development](grant-proposal-development.qmd) and [Manuscript Peer Review](manuscript-peer-review.qmd) for worked examples.

Public-health bodies (WHO, ASPPH) offer frameworks for AI ethics and governance. They translate values — accountability, transparency, equity, safety — into checkable practices.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Moderate — Write a disclosure norm</summary>
```

**Time** ~15 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Decide where and how to disclose AI use.

**Setup.** Below are three role-specific lists of places AI commonly shows up at DSPH. Find the list closest to your role:

**Admin staff:**

- Drafting email reminders about deadlines (reaccreditation, HR, events)
- Summarizing long policy documents or meeting minutes
- Reformatting data from spreadsheets into reports or slide decks
- Writing first drafts of job postings or committee charges

**Research faculty/staff:**

- Summarizing literature or generating search strategies
- Brainstorming and critiquing grant aims (not drafting them — see NIH/NSF policy), drafting abstracts or progress reports
- Generating or cleaning code (R, Stata, Python)
- Producing tables, figures, or lay summaries from results

**Teaching faculty:**

- Drafting syllabi, rubrics, or assignment descriptions
- Creating quiz questions or case studies
- Writing recommendation letters or advising notes
- Generating lecture outlines or discussion prompts

Pick the three uses from your list that feel most relevant. If none fits, substitute your own.

**Steps.**

1. For each of your three uses, decide: Should you disclose AI assistance? To whom? How? (A footnote? A verbal mention? Not at all?)

2. Draft a norm:

   > Draft a one-paragraph AI-use disclosure norm for a public-health school covering these three cases. Be balanced and practical — neither requiring disclosure for every spell-check nor allowing silence on consequential uses. Cases: [your three uses].

3. Refine to fit your context. A good norm should pass the "new colleague" test — could someone who just joined your team follow it without ambiguity?

**Expected result.** A short, shareable disclosure norm that distinguishes trivial use (no disclosure needed) from consequential use (disclose).

**Check your work.** Is it specific enough to act on, or just aspirational? "Use AI responsibly" is not a norm. "Disclose AI assistance in any document submitted externally — grants, publications, accreditation reports — by noting the tool and the human verification step" is.

**Common pitfalls.** Over-disclosing trivial use (auto-complete, grammar check) is noise; under-disclosing consequential use (drafting a grant aim, writing a student recommendation) is a problem. Aim for "where it matters to the reader."

**Stretch (optional).** Add a line on who's accountable when AI informs a decision, and circulate the draft to one colleague for feedback.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Moderate — Apply a governance framework</summary>
```

**Time** ~15 min · **Tools** WHO or ASPPH framework + ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Turn principles into practice.

**Setup.** Skim one framework (e.g., WHO ethics and governance of AI for health).

**Steps.**

1. Pick two principles (e.g., transparency, equity).

2. Make them concrete:

   > For each principle, give two concrete actions a public-health team could take in everyday AI use. [principles]

3. Adapt the actions to a real task you do.

**Expected result.** Two principles turned into actions you'd actually take.

**Check your work.** Could you tell whether you're following the action, or is it vague?

**Common pitfalls.** Frameworks become wallpaper if not operationalized. Make them checkable.

**Stretch (optional).** Identify one current practice that violates a principle and fix it.

```{=html}
</details>
```


## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "The governing accountability principle is…", "options": ["The person who acts on AI output owns the result", "The vendor is liable", "The tool decides", "No one is responsible"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Responsibility stays with the human."}, {"q": "Disclosure of AI use should be…", "options": ["Where it informs a real decision or document", "Never", "Always, for everything", "Only if asked"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Disclose where it matters to the decision."}, {"q": "Governance frameworks help by…", "options": ["Turning values into checkable practices", "Banning AI", "Replacing judgment", "Adding paperwork only"], "answer": 0, "explain": "They operationalize principles."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

Available in the shared OneDrive folder **Staff Faculty AI Workshop → Readings**, and online where linked:

- [**How Responsible AI Changes in the Agent Era**](https://www.turingpost.com/p/how-responsible-ai-changes-in-the-agent-era) — responsibility as AI gains autonomy.
- [**Prioritizing the Risks from Artificial Intelligence**](https://osf.io/pj2qr) — a framework for weighing AI risks.

## Useful resources

- [WHO: Ethics & Governance of AI for Health](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240029200) — the leading health-sector framework.
- [ChatGPT Edu (Drexel AI Tools)](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — the approved tool, with data rules.
- [Grant Proposal Development](grant-proposal-development.qmd) — disclosure norms grounded in actual funder policy (NIH NOT-OD-25-132, NSF).
- [Manuscript Peer Review](manuscript-peer-review.qmd) — disclosure norms grounded in journal policy (ICMJE, COPE).

---

## Navigate

**Next in this track:** [AI Bias & Health Equity Audit](bias-equity-audit.qmd)

**Related topics:** [AI Bias & Health Equity Audit](bias-equity-audit.qmd) · [Verifying AI Output](verifying-output.qmd)

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Responsible use includes the data rules — confirm classification before any real data. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
